title: The Nonpareil Combed Pattern
subtitle: Building a classic tight-wave marbled pattern from stone drops
excerpt: The nonpareil is the most recognisable of the traditional combed marbled patterns: a regular field of fine wavy lines produced by raking a stone drop pattern in two perpendicular passes. It is an ideal introduction to combed marbling because the result makes clear exactly how the tools interact with the size.
sourceNotes: Halfer, Josef. Progress of the Marbling Art. English translation. Buffalo, NY: O.H. Neumann, 1893/1894. Chapter on comb patterns.

glossaryTerms.definition: A combed marbled pattern characterised by a tight, even wave across the whole sheet, produced by raking a stone pattern first with a wide-toothed rake and then with a fine-toothed rake at right angles.
The basic marbled pattern formed by dropping colours onto the size surface. Each drop pushes the preceding drops outward, producing concentric circles or irregular lobes depending on drop sequence.

The gel-like liquid base for Western marbling, made from carrageenan seaweed extract dissolved in water. The size surface tension supports the floating paint and allows it to be manipulated before printing.

attrs.heading: Marbling chemistry safety
attrs.body: Alum is a mild skin and eye irritant. Wear gloves when mixing or applying the mordant and rinse splashes promptly. Carrageenan is non-toxic but spilled size is slippery; wipe spills immediately. Do not eat or drink at the marbling tray.

content.text: Preparing the carrageenan size

Dissolve carrageenan powder in cold water according to the supplier's proportion (typically 1 to 2 g per litre). Blend with a stick blender until fully incorporated and leave to stand for at least two hours, or overnight. The
carrageenan size
should be clear and have a slightly gel-like consistency: not stiff, but with enough body to hold a drop of paint on the surface without it sinking immediately.

Pour the size into the marbling tray to a depth of at least 4 to 5 cm. Skim the surface to remove bubbles: draw a strip of newspaper across the surface and discard it. Repeat until the surface is clear.
Mordanting the paper

Alum solution is the mordant that allows the acrylic-based marbling paint to transfer from the size surface to the paper and adhere permanently. Dissolve potash alum in warm water at approximately 30 g per litre. Apply this solution to the paper using a sponge or foam brush, coating both sides for best results. Allow to dry completely.

Alum-treated paper must be used the same day; paper left overnight after alum treatment can become brittle. Mark the treated side with a light pencil tick on a corner so you always know which side goes face-down onto the size.
Dropping the stone pattern
The nonpareil begins as a
stone pattern

. Load the stylus or dropper with the first colour. Hold it about 5 to 8 cm above the size surface and allow drops to fall freely onto it. The drop will spread outward in a circle. Each subsequent drop of a different colour pushes the previous drops further outward, producing the characteristic concentric ring arrangement.

For the nonpareil, drop all colours across the whole tray surface in a relatively even distribution. The goal is to cover the tray with no large areas of bare size. Typical practice is four to six colours, alternating between a dark and a light in the drop sequence to create contrast at every circle boundary.

Drop density for the nonpareil should be higher than for a simple stone pattern displayed as finished work. The subsequent raking will compress the drops, and a sparse stone will produce an uneven nonpareil with gaps.
The wide rake pass

With the stone drops covering the tray, pass the wide-toothed rake (typically 2 to 3 cm between teeth) once along the length of the tray, starting from one short end and moving evenly to the other. Keep the rake teeth just below the surface of the size; dragging them through the surface rather than skimming on top of it.

Speed matters: too fast and the colours streak; too slow and the colours blend where the teeth pass through. A moderate, walking-pace movement is correct. The result is a series of undulating stripes running the length of the tray.
The fine rake pass at right angles

Rotate 90 degrees relative to the first pass. Draw the fine-toothed rake (typically 5 to 8 mm between teeth) across the width of the tray in a single pass at the same speed and depth as the first. This second pass cuts across the stripes from the first pass, pulling each one into a series of tight waves.

The fine rake must pass the full width without lifting or pausing mid-way. A hesitation creates a visible jump in the wave pattern. The finished surface should show a regular, fine grid of colour waves: the
nonpareil
pattern.
Printing

Hold the alum-treated paper at two opposite corners, alum side facing down. Lower the paper onto the size surface in a single controlled movement: let one edge touch first, then roll the paper down to lay flat without trapping air bubbles. Leave for 30 seconds. Lift from one corner in a smooth, steady pull.

Carry the sheet to the rinse area and rinse the front surface gently under cold running water, or dip into a tray of clean water. The excess size and unfixed paint will wash off; the mordanted paint remains. Pin or hang the sheet to dry. Skim the remaining paint from the size surface before pulling the next sheet.

items.symptom: Paint sinks rather than floating on the size
items.cause: Size too thin, paint too heavy, or paint needs more surfactant (ox gall)
items.fix: Check size consistency; add a drop of ox gall to each colour and test on a spare corner of size before dropping the full pattern.

items.symptom: Colour does not transfer to paper
items.cause: Alum treatment too old, applied too thinly, or the wrong side of the paper was placed down
items.fix: Re-treat paper with fresh alum solution; confirm which side was treated before printing.

items.cause: Rake lifted mid-pass, uneven rake speed, or stone drops too sparse
items.fix: Practise a single clean rake pass without stopping; increase drop density before the next pull.
